item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this is necroposting, but can you please have the list of noteable species be a collaboration from the readers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My personal favorite scp. Could you create a log of species that anyone can add too, similar to the one of notable species? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really like this one. Jellyfish Island! :D +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the point of having the SCPS ships have completely different cover names? That seems like a completely pointless security measure that would only cause problems later on—-as in people working on a ship being asked what ship they serve on, and giving them the internal name instead of the external name by accident.

item.title: A small question
item.content:encoded: The footnote observes that this SCP is suspected not to affect asexual organisms, and yet the palm trees are specifically called out as having developed an asexual reproductive method. It could be that this is resolved by their subsequent extinction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember reading this in chat and when it was first posted and liking it, dunno why I hadn't upvoted til now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incredible for a first SCP. You certainly know the science behind what you're dealing with here! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a Marine Biology major about to take a Behavioral and Evolutionary Biology final, I approve.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why did it take me so long to read this. This is fantastic. +1 / +1 would read again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite a stellar effort here, especially for a first SCP. It's imaginative, manages to use biology and evolution in a non-offensive way and is all around a pleasant read. While I did notice some very minor tonal hiccups, this is a definite upvote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty cool. Great first article. Hope to see more from you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Usually, leviathans of the deep are frightening as all get-out, but this one has a certain whimsicality despite its mass. I dig this, and I like that's contained in the Gyre.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So everything with the present tense used in the description there is still around? Neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Floating island jellyfish of evolution? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No worries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one does it for me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wartheim succintly sums up exactly how I feel about this SCP. A good start to contribution on the site. Not a racist skunk. For all these reasons and more, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough, I didn't mean to sound rude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes I am aware that there are single-celled organisms that reproduce sexually but *shhhhhhhhhhhh*

If you're talking about diatoms then you're in the clear anyway since the researchers wouldn't be likely to bring them along without intending to and the naturally occurring ones probably wouldn't stay within the effect range long enough for it to really matter.

azzleflux,

This was my first post on this site after being away for a short while and posting on forums that don't allow you to edit posts, so it felt natural adding a reply rather than editing. I remembered I could edit posts, but only after I posted the second one and since I'm not a mod there was no way to get rid of the second post. Will edit posts in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All valid points. You're correct in assuming that there is some force that prohibits negative mutations, its not addressed because there's really no way to explain it in any satisfying way that does not include magic/comic book physics. I'll add a line that notes the lack of negative mutations.

As for research personnel, the effects of the island only extend to species that actually reproduce on or within a certain range of the island. I'll say that this is because the effect actually takes hold during the fertilization process, if only to avoid the question as to whether or not gametes are affected. Go ahead and include that in your head canon because I don't really feel it needs mentioning, or at least I could include it in a way that doesn't take away more than it adds.

I did think about microbes, and I think I actually solved my own problem just now. If the effect takes place during fertilization, then an asexually reproducing organism would not be affected. so maybe I will add that after all. (yes I am aware that there are single-celled organisms that reproduce sexually but *shhhhhhhhhhhh*)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey man, you obviously know how to edit your posts, so could you do that from now on instead of replying to yourself?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oooooo. Me likey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This could be fixed with a footnote.

I really like this but it has a notable number of small grammatical errors - mostly commas and such. I'd be happy to fix it up for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
apparent age (Estimated at over 6000 years)

Fix the capitalization there.

Otherwise, I really like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, just thought of this: you mentioned that there are Bi-monthly research excursions to the SCP; would there be any special containment procedures involving subsequent reproduction of research personnel? (ie, barred from having children, must be sterile/have a vasectomy, take future kids in to be examined, etc.)

I'm guessing this would depend on whether the anomalous evolution rate is effected when organisms reproduce in the vicinity of the SCP or the SCP affects individual organisms which then go on to reproduce. (Both would satisfy the "effect stops away from the SCP" requirement, at least so far as the foundation could tell.)

Edit: Also, what about microbes on the researchers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While it is good that you have the "rapid evolution" occurring across generations rather than within a single organism, it kind of leaves an unanswered question (not sure if this is intentional or not.)

Normally with faster evolution you would have either shorter population times or an increased rate of mutation (due to radiation or being error-prone at copying DNA like bacteria or something else.) In your SCP it's the latter.; the issue is that with increased mutations you would be as likely if not more likely to accrue detrimental(read: kills it dead)/irrelevant mutations as you would beneficial ones. And even with beneficial mutations they would have to be beneficial mutations that don't prevent the organism from mating with the rest of its population. There's a reason the vast majority of organisms have adapted processes that limit mutations from one generation to the next.

So if the evolving-faster-than-normal species aren't ending up gone in couple of generations, there has to be a reason. The organisms being highly fecund could be a way around this, which would also entail many individuals of each species dying off/failing to reproduce each generation due to detrimental mutation. The other obvious reason (and I think this is what you were thinking of when you wrote this) is that, for whatever reason, detrimental mutations hardly ever occur on this jellyfish island.

If it's the latter, it necessitates that there is some force/process/entity preventing detrimental mutations. You don't need to explain it or say that there must be something causing it, but if this is the case the fact that there are only beneficial mutations observed taking place on the island should definitely get a mention somewhere in the SCP, because an area where no detrimental mutations occur is highly anomalous and a study team would definitely take notice.

Overall I liked it, +1. It's a neat concept and you fleshed out the anomalous hanger-ons well.

Also, just a fun bit of info if you didn't know already: while not a coconut palm, there is a palm tree that reproduces asexually (kinda.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad for a first SCP. I can't really say much for also being quite new. But I think it's good for a start. Kudos to you +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unique, extensive, and scientifically "proper".

Not jaw-droppingly spectacular, but competent at being interesting. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm feeling like this isn't really contained.

I'm feeling that to "really contain" this thing would require too many resources; they had a difficult enough time building a giant pool to film Titanic, imagine making a holding tank to contain something a couple kilometers long!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this be Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A recruitment surface is any area suitable for a sessile organism (oyster, mussel, coral, sponge) to attach to, and form the foundation for reef-building organisms to begin building.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A nice, simple object that's still interesting. And kudos on avoiding the "and one of the species is of human like intelligence" pitfall/cliche.

One thing:

recruitment surface

What does that mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dig it. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What?

SCP-1585 is evolving!

(evolution music)

Congratulations! Your SCP-1585 evolved into an Upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of an island sized jellyfish as is, and the fact that you used evolution correctly makes me happier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had a hard time deciding whether it should be safe or euclid, to be honest. If other people think it should change then I'll change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
''Subsequent generations are not known to retain their rapid evolutionary pace once leaving SCP-1585, although, their specialized adaptions often makes them a threat to local ecology.''

This doesn't seem Safe to me. It's unpredictable, isn't really contained, and has the opportunity of evolving some really nasty stuff (I'm thinking microbes). Euclid seems more appropriate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What else are they supposed to do? This way, it's not disrupting normality or hurting anyone at all, so I'd call it contained.

Also, this tickles my biology bone a lot :D it's really neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty damn neato. A giant living evolving island. It's not superduper amazing unique awesome, but it's a solid first attempt. Good work, I hope it survives without people pecking through its bones nitpicking it to death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
that is… unique.
at least you got evolution right, not individual animals evolving.
it would help if you cleared up some of the latin names to be in english first, then nomenclature, that would be helpful.
seems cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It probably doesn't matter, but I'm feeling like this isn't really contained. There's just a bunch of dudes that go watch it forever. Also, the bit about "rapid evolution" seems a bit tacked-on. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First SCP, Let me know what you think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it in forums, liked it in chat, like it on the mainlist. +1
